title: Why "Just Try Harder" Doesn't Work for Anxious Kids : what the pediatrician usually misses

body:
# Why "Just Try Harder" Doesn't Work for Anxious Kids : what the pediatrician usually misses

*TL;DR: Pediatricians often dismiss childhood anxiety as a lack of effort. They tell parents to push harder. That advice ignores the biology. Anxiety isn't laziness. It's a nervous system hijack. Here's what your pediatrician probably missed, and what you can do instead.*

Your pediatrician told you your child just needs to try harder. That advice is not just unhelpful. It's biologically wrong.

Let me be straight with you. You've probably heard it in that well-lit exam room. The doctor smiles. Says something like "She's just shy. She'll grow out of it." Or "He needs to face his fears. Push him a little."

You nod. You feel relieved. Someone with a medical degree confirmed what you secretly feared: you've been too soft.

Look, here's the thing. That pediatrician means well. But they're working from a training manual that hasn't been updated since the 1990s. Most medical schools spend less than two hours on childhood anxiety. Two. Hours.

So what do they know? They know how to check ears. They know vaccine schedules. They know growth charts.

They don't know how an anxious child's brain actually works.

, -

## The Pediatrician's Blind Spot: Training vs. Reality

Let me demystify this for you. Pediatricians are trained in medicine. Not in child psychology. Not in neuroscience. Not in the subtle differences between introversion, shyness, and clinical anxiety.

The standard advice "just try harder" comes from a model of willpower. It assumes your child is choosing to avoid. That they could do it if they really wanted to.

Here's what actually works: understanding that anxiety is a physiological state. You can't will yourself out of a fight-or-flight response any more than you can will yourself out of a fever.

Nobody's coming to explain this to you. So I will.

The pediatrician missed three things. Let me walk you through each one.

, -

## What Pediatricians Usually Miss About Anxiety

### Miss #1: The Difference Between Shyness and Anxiety

Shyness is a personality trait. Anxiety is a disorder. They look similar from the outside. Both involve withdrawal. Both involve silence. Both involve avoidance.

Inside the body, they're completely different.

Shyness feels like hesitation. Like wanting to join but feeling awkward. The nervous system is calm. The child just lacks social practice.

Anxiety feels like drowning. Like standing on a cliff edge. The heart races. The stomach lurches. The brain shouts "Danger!" even when there's none.

Your pediatrician probably lumped them together. That's a mistake.

### Miss #2: The Role of Sensory Sensitivity

Elaine Aron's research on highly sensitive children shows that about 20% of kids have a nervous system that processes stimuli more deeply. They notice more. They react more. They get overwhelmed faster.

The school wasn't built for your child. That's not your child's fault.

Pediatricians rarely ask about sensory triggers. They don't connect the dots between a child who can't tolerate loud classrooms and a child who refuses to go to school. But they're the same kid.

### Miss #3: The Recharge Time Fallacy

You know that crash after school? The meltdown over homework? The sudden tears when you ask "How was your day?"

Pediatricians often see this as defiance. Or exhaustion from a long day. They tell parents to enforce a stricter routine.

The recharge time after school isn't laziness. It's biology.

An anxious child's nervous system runs hot all day. They're scanning for threats. They're managing sensory input. They're holding it together. When they get home, the system crashes because it's finally safe.

This isn't mystical. It's mechanical.

, -

## The Biology Behind "I Can't"

Stop overthinking this. It's simple. Your child's amygdala is doing its job too well.

The amygdala is the brain's smoke detector. In anxious kids, it's hypersensitive. It goes off for false alarms. A mildly difficult math problem? Fire alarm. A group of classmates whispering? Fire alarm. A schedule change? Fire alarm.

When the amygdala fires, the prefrontal cortex goes offline. That's the rational part. It's the part that says "I can do this" or "That's not a real threat."

So when your child says "I can't," they're not lying. Their prefrontal cortex is literally shut down. They can't access the part of their brain that would help them cope.

Telling them to try harder is like telling someone with a broken leg to walk it off. The equipment isn't working.

Jerome Kagan's longitudinal studies on inhibited temperament showed that these kids have a different baseline physiology. Higher heart rates. More cortisol. A more reactive sympathetic nervous system from infancy.

This isn't bad parenting. It's not a character flaw. It's biology.

, -

## Three Things That Actually Work (Instead of "Try Harder")

### Stop the pep talks

You already know the answer. You just don't like it. Pep talks make things worse.

When you say "You'll be fine! Just go in there!" your child hears: "You're wrong to feel scared." That increases shame. Shame amplifies anxiety.

Try this instead: "I see you're scared. That makes sense. Let's figure out a plan."

Validation lowers the arousal. The amygdala needs to hear "You are safe" from someone it trusts. It can't hear "It's not that bad" until the threat response drops.

### Use the body

Anxiety lives in the body. Not in the thoughts. So interventions must start with the body.

Breathing exercises help. But not the "take a deep breath" you do in yoga. That can actually make things worse for some kids because it increases oxygen too fast.

Try the 4-7-8 breath. Inhale for 4. Hold for 7. Exhale for 8. It activates the parasympathetic nervous system. It physically forces the vagus nerve to calm down.

Better yet: movement. Jumping jacks. Running in place. Pushing against a wall. Any rhythmic, repetitive physical activity helps discharge cortisol.

### Create a "reset" ritual after school

Here's what actually works. A 20-minute buffer between school and homework. No questions. No demands. No eye contact if that's too much.

Let your child decompress in their own way. Some kids need a snack and a show. Some need silence. Some need to build with Legos.

The school day is a sensory assault for anxious kids. They need time to let the smoke clear before they're expected to function again.

Dawn Huebner calls this "decompression time." Natasha Daniels calls it "the afternoon meltdown zone." Whatever you call it, respect it.

, -

## When to Push and When to Pause

Here's the part everyone gets wrong. Some anxiety requires gentle exposure. Some needs complete retreat.

The rule of thumb: If your child is in a panic attack, do not push. The amygdala is already on fire. Adding pressure makes the smoke detector scream louder.

If your child is avoiding something out of habit, not terror, then gentle exposure helps. The key word is gentle. Not "you're doing it now." Not "I'm not giving you a choice." More like "Let's sit in the car outside the party for five minutes. Then we decide."

Pediatricians often give blanket advice: "Push through it." That's dangerous for some kids. It can create learned helplessness. It can strengthen the fear pathway.

Wendy Mogel calls this "making a roadmap instead of giving a push." You show your child the route. You let them walk it at their own pace.

You know your child best. The pediatrician doesn't live in your house. Trust your gut. If something feels wrong, it probably is.

, -

## FAQ

**Q: My pediatrician said my child is just being manipulative. Is that possible?**

No. Manipulation requires intentional awareness of cause and effect. Anxious children are not thinking "I'll refuse school so Mom feels sorry for me." They're thinking "If I go to school, something terrible might happen and I can't survive it." That's not manipulation. That's survival.

**Q: Should I get a second opinion?**

If your pediatrician dismissed your concerns or offered only "try harder" advice, yes. Look for a child psychologist or a pediatrician with a specialty in behavioral health. The American Academy of Child and Adolescent Psychiatry has a find-a-doctor tool.

**Q: Can medication help?**

For some children, yes. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) are effective for moderate to severe anxiety. Talk to a child psychiatrist. The pediatrician may not have the expertise to prescribe these correctly.

**Q: How do I explain this to teachers who say my child is lazy?**

Short answer: "My child has an anxiety disorder. It's not about effort. Here's what helps them." Offer concrete strategies. Teachers respond better to specific requests than to explanations. The CDC has a good handout on classroom accommodations for anxiety.

, -

The next time someone tells you your child just needs to try harder, remember: they're wrong. Not because you're soft. Not because your child is weak. But because the biology doesn't work that way.

You don't need to fix your child. You need to understand what's actually going on. And you need people in your corner who get it.

If you're tired of explaining this to pediatricians, teachers, and even your own family, you're not alone. The Oracle Lover is here for you. A whole community of parents who know the difference between anxiety and defiance. Between sensitivity and weakness. Between trying harder and trying smarter.

[anxiety vs defiance guide](/articles/anxiety-as-a-qualifying-disability--for-fifth-grade-parents)
[school anxiety survival kit](/articles/anxiety-as-a-qualifying-disability--for-middle-school-parents)
[how to talk to pediatrician about anxiety](/articles/anxiety-as-a-qualifying-disability--for-fifth-grade-parents)

You already know your child better than anyone. Trust that. Then find the right tools.

*Lokah samastah sukhino bhavantu.*

*Sat Chit Ananda.*
